 {"id":"bd-2d66","title":"fix: use saturating arithmetic in virtualized render_end calculation","description":"In virtualized.rs line 840-841, the render_end calculation uses unchecked addition: (scroll_offset + items_per_viewport + overscan).min(total_items). While overflow is unlikely in practice, using saturating_add would be more defensive and consistent with the rest of the codebase.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-02-02T18:21:29.707761812Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-02T18:22:11.130653494Z","closed_at":"2026-02-02T18:22:11.130634058Z","close_reason":"Already fixed: render_end uses saturating_add; verified in virtualized.rs","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"bd-2dc","title":"Implement Status Line and Panel widgets","description":"PHASE 5 - STATUS LINE + PANEL WIDGETS

## Background
Status lines and panels are essential for agent harness UIs. Status line shows current mode/status. Panel provides structured chrome around content areas.

## Technical Requirements

### Status Line Widget

pub struct StatusLine<'a> {
    left: Vec<StatusItem<'a>>,
    center: Vec<StatusItem<'a>>,
    right: Vec<StatusItem<'a>>,
    style: Style,
    separator: &'a str,
}

pub enum StatusItem<'a> {
    Text(Text<'a>),
    Spinner(SpinnerState),
    Progress { current: u64, total: u64 },
    KeyHint { key: &'a str, action: &'a str },
    Spacer,
}

impl StatusLine<'_> {
    pub fn left(mut self, item: StatusItem) -> Self;
    pub fn center(mut self, item: StatusItem) -> Self;
    pub fn right(mut self, item: StatusItem) -> Self;
}

// Typical usage
StatusLine::new()
    .left(StatusItem::Text("[INSERT]".into()))
    .center(StatusItem::Text("file.rs".into()))
    .right(StatusItem::KeyHint { key: "^C", action: "Quit" })
    .right(StatusItem::Text("Ln 42, Col 10".into()))

### Panel Widget

pub struct Panel<'a> {
    title: Option<Title<'a>>,
    content: Box<dyn Widget + 'a>,
    footer: Option<Text<'a>>,
    border_style: BorderStyle,
    focus_style: Option<Style>,
    focused: bool,
}

impl Panel<'_> {
    pub fn new(content: impl Widget) -> Self;
    pub fn title(mut self, title: impl Into<Title>) -> Self;
    pub fn footer(mut self, footer: impl Into<Text>) -> Self;
    pub fn focused(mut self, focused: bool) -> Self;
}

### Layout Integration
Panels work with Flex/Grid:

Flex::vertical()
    .add(Panel::new(log_viewer).title("Logs").flex(1.0))
    .add(Panel::new(input).title("Command").height(3))
    .add(StatusLine::new()...)

### Focus Management
- focused property changes border style
- Tab navigation between panels (runtime handles)
- Focus indicator (optional marker)

## Tests Required
- [ ] StatusLine left/center/right positioning
- [ ] StatusLine separator rendering
- [ ] StatusLine truncation behavior
- [ ] Panel border rendering
- [ ] Panel title positioning
- [ ] Panel focus style change
- [ ] Panel with various content types
- [ ] Empty status line

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] StatusLine with 3 regions
- [ ] StatusItem variants
- [ ] Panel wrapper widget
- [ ] Focus styling
- [ ] Integration with layouts

-{"id":"bd-2dow","title":"Widget Intrinsic Sizing System (Widget::measure() Trait)","description":"## Goal
Enable widgets to report their intrinsic (preferred/minimum/maximum) dimensions, unlocking content-aware layout like CSS flexbox/Tailwind.

## Background & Motivation
Currently, FrankenTUI widgets receive a `Rect` and render into it, but they cannot communicate their sizing preferences back to the layout system. This is a fundamental limitation that prevents:
- **Shrink-to-fit containers**: A button can't say 'I need 12 chars wide'
- **Content-based sizing**: A paragraph can't report its wrapped height
- **Min/max constraints**: Widgets can't enforce minimum viable sizes
- **Auto-sizing in flex layouts**: Constraint::Min/Max work on ratios, not content

CSS flexbox and Tailwind achieve reflowability by having elements report intrinsic sizes (min-content, max-content, fit-content). We need the same.

## Design Approach
Introduce a `MeasurableWidget` trait (or extend `Widget`) with:
```rust
pub trait MeasurableWidget: Widget {
    /// Compute preferred size given available space
    fn measure(&self, available: Size) -> Size;
    
    /// Minimum size below which widget is unusable
    fn min_size(&self) -> Size { Size::ZERO }
    
    /// Maximum size beyond which more space is wasted
    fn max_size(&self) -> Option<Size> { None }
}
```

## Scope
- Core trait definition and semantics
- Default implementations for existing widgets
- Layout solver integration (Constraint::FitContent)
- Caching/memoization of measure results
- Migration path for custom widgets

## Non-Goals
- GPU-accelerated layout (separate concern)
- CSS-compatible box model (we're terminal-native)

## Acceptance Criteria
- All 37 core widgets implement MeasurableWidget
- Layout solver uses intrinsic sizes for Min/FitContent constraints
- Measure results are cached and invalidated correctly
- No performance regression (lazy evaluation)
- Demo showcase demonstrates content-aware layouts

## Tests / Validation
- Unit tests for measure/min/max outputs across core widgets.
- Property tests: monotonicity with available size; min <= preferred <= max.
- Snapshot tests: content-aware layouts in the demo harness.
- E2E PTY script with JSONL logging of sizes, constraints, chosen intrinsic sizes, and checksums.

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Intrinsic sizing is usable end-to-end: widget impls + solver integration + caching + demo.
- [ ] Test suite provides strong guarantees (unit/property/snapshot + deterministic PTY E2E).
- [ ] Performance is budgeted and regressions are caught early (bench + CI gates).
- [ ] Migration guidance enables external widget authors to implement measurement correctly.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-02T23:23:34.866455533Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-03T17:44:17.304246589Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["api","core","layout"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2dow","depends_on_id":"bd-1rz0.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-03T14:32:17Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":362,"issue_id":"bd-2dow","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Architectural Context

### Current State
The existing Widget trait is simple:
```rust
pub trait Widget {
    fn render(&self, area: Rect, frame: &mut Frame);
    fn is_essential(&self) -> bool { false }
}
```

Widgets are 'told' their size by the layout system. They have no voice in the matter.

### The Problem
Consider a responsive dashboard with flexible panels. Currently:
1. Layout solver allocates space based on Constraint ratios
2. Widget receives Rect and renders
3. If content doesn't fit, it truncates or overflows
4. There's no feedback loop to inform better layout decisions

With intrinsic sizing:
1. Layout solver queries widgets for preferred sizes
2. Solver uses these as inputs to constraint resolution
3. Widgets receive well-sized Rects that respect their needs
4. Content naturally fits without truncation

### Why This Matters for Zero-Flicker Reflow
During resize, the layout solver needs to make fast, correct decisions. Without intrinsic sizes, it can only use static ratios. With intrinsic sizes, it can:
- Collapse non-essential widgets when space is tight
- Expand widgets up to their max-content
- Prioritize essential content in constrained layouts

### Relationship to Other Beads
- **bd-1rz0 (Responsive Reflow)**: Intrinsic sizing provides the 'content signal' the reflow engine needs
- **bd-4kq0 (Kernel Optimization)**: Measure caching prevents redundant computation
- **bd-1rz0.5 (Responsive Layout Primitives)**: Breakpoints + intrinsic sizing = full responsive design

### Implementation Strategy
1. Define trait with sensible defaults (min=0, max=unbounded)
2. Implement for simple widgets first (Paragraph, Block, Span)
3. Add layout solver integration (new Constraint::FitContent)
4. Implement for complex widgets (List, Table, Tabs)
5. Add caching layer with invalidation
6. Update demo showcase with examples

## Integration Checkpoints

 {"id":"bd-2dow.1","title":"MeasurableWidget Trait Definition + Semantics Spec","description":"## Goal
Define the MeasurableWidget trait with precise semantics, documented invariants, and sensible defaults.

## Deliverables
1. Trait definition in ftui-widgets/src/lib.rs (or dedicated module)
2. SizeConstraints type for rich size information
3. Comprehensive rustdoc with examples
4. Invariant documentation
5. Unit and property tests

## Trait Design (Enhanced)
```rust
/// Size constraints returned by measure operations.
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, Hash)]
pub struct SizeConstraints {
    /// Minimum size below which widget is unusable/clips content
    pub min: Size,
    /// Preferred size that best displays content
    pub preferred: Size,
    /// Maximum useful size (beyond this, space is wasted)
    pub max: Option<Size>,
}

impl SizeConstraints {
    pub const ZERO: Self = Self { min: Size::ZERO, preferred: Size::ZERO, max: None };
    
    /// Clamp a given size to these constraints
    pub fn clamp(&self, size: Size) -> Size {
        let max = self.max.unwrap_or(Size::MAX);
        Size {
            width: size.width.clamp(self.min.width, max.width),
            height: size.height.clamp(self.min.height, max.height),
        }
    }
}

/// A widget that can report its intrinsic dimensions.
pub trait MeasurableWidget: Widget {
    /// Measure the widget given available space.
    /// 
    /// # Arguments
    /// - `available`: Maximum space the widget could occupy
    /// 
    /// # Returns
    /// SizeConstraints describing min/preferred/max sizes
    /// 
    /// # Invariants
    /// - min <= preferred <= max.unwrap_or(∞)
    /// - Must be pure: same input → same output
    /// - Should be O(content_length) worst case
    fn measure(&self, available: Size) -> SizeConstraints;
    
    /// Quick check: does this widget have content-dependent sizing?
    /// Widgets returning false can skip measure() calls when only chrome matters.
    fn has_intrinsic_size(&self) -> bool { true }
}

// Blanket impl for backwards compatibility
impl<T: Widget> MeasurableWidget for T {
    default fn measure(&self, _available: Size) -> SizeConstraints {
        SizeConstraints::ZERO // Fill available space
    }
    
    default fn has_intrinsic_size(&self) -> bool { false }
}
```

## Open Questions (Resolved)
1. ✅ Should measure take &self or &mut self? → &self for purity
2. ✅ Stateful widgets? → Measure reflects current state; cache invalidates on state change
3. ✅ SizeHint enum? → Yes, via SizeConstraints struct

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests (crates/ftui-widgets/src/measurable/tests.rs)
```rust
#[test]
fn size_constraints_clamp_works() {
    let c = SizeConstraints { min: Size::new(5, 2), preferred: Size::new(10, 5), max: Some(Size::new(20, 10)) };
    assert_eq!(c.clamp(Size::new(3, 1)), Size::new(5, 2));  // Below min
    assert_eq!(c.clamp(Size::new(15, 7)), Size::new(15, 7)); // In range
    assert_eq!(c.clamp(Size::new(30, 20)), Size::new(20, 10)); // Above max
}

#[test]
fn default_impl_returns_zero() {
    struct PlainWidget;
    impl Widget for PlainWidget { fn render(&self, _: Rect, _: &mut Frame) {} }
    assert_eq!(PlainWidget.measure(Size::MAX), SizeConstraints::ZERO);
    assert!(!PlainWidget.has_intrinsic_size());
}
```

### Property Tests (using proptest)
```rust
proptest! {
    #[test]
    fn measure_invariant_min_le_preferred(available in size_strategy()) {
        let widget = TestParagraph::new("test");
        let c = widget.measure(available);
        prop_assert!(c.min.width <= c.preferred.width);
        prop_assert!(c.min.height <= c.preferred.height);
    }
    
    #[test]
    fn measure_invariant_preferred_le_max(available in size_strategy()) {
        let widget = TestParagraph::new("test");
        let c = widget.measure(available);
        if let Some(max) = c.max {
            prop_assert!(c.preferred.width <= max.width);
            prop_assert!(c.preferred.height <= max.height);
        }
    }
    
    #[test]
    fn measure_is_pure(available in size_strategy()) {
        let widget = TestParagraph::new("hello world");
        let a = widget.measure(available);
        let b = widget.measure(available);
        prop_assert_eq!(a, b);
    }
}
```

## Files to Modify
- crates/ftui-widgets/src/lib.rs (re-export)
- crates/ftui-widgets/src/measurable.rs (new: trait + SizeConstraints)
- crates/ftui-widgets/src/measurable/tests.rs (new: unit + property tests)
- crates/ftui-core/src/geometry.rs (if Size::MAX needed)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] SizeConstraints type with min/preferred/max and clamp()
- [ ] MeasurableWidget trait with measure() and has_intrinsic_size()
- [ ] Blanket impl provides backwards-compatible defaults
- [ ] Comprehensive rustdoc with examples
- [ ] Unit tests for SizeConstraints
- [ ] Property tests for trait invariants
- [ ] All tests pass: cargo test -p ftui-widgets","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-02T23:24:29.727608291Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-03T00:25:22.926944794Z","closed_at":"2026-02-03T00:25:22.926904959Z","close_reason":"Implemented MeasurableWidget trait and SizeConstraints type with comprehensive tests","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["api","core","spec"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2dow.1","depends_on_id":"bd-2dow","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-03T14:32:17Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-2dow.2","title":"Implement MeasurableWidget for Simple Widgets (Block, Paragraph, Span)","description":"## Goal
Implement MeasurableWidget for the foundational widgets that most others compose.

## Widgets in Scope
1. **Block** - Border + padding contribute fixed overhead; may wrap inner content
2. **Paragraph** - Text wrapping determines height given width
3. **Span** - Single-line text with known width
4. **Clear** - Always fills available (has_intrinsic_size = false)
5. **Line** (horizontal/vertical) - Fixed 1-cell thickness

## Implementation Details

### Block
```rust
impl MeasurableWidget for Block<'_> {
    fn measure(&self, available: Size) -> SizeConstraints {
        let chrome = self.chrome_size(); // borders + padding + title
        
        // Inner available after chrome
        let inner_available = Size {
            width: available.width.saturating_sub(chrome.width),
            height: available.height.saturating_sub(chrome.height),
        };
        
        // If inner widget is measurable, query it
        let inner_constraints = self.inner
            .as_ref()
            .map(|w| w.measure(inner_available))
            .unwrap_or(SizeConstraints::ZERO);
        
        SizeConstraints {
            min: Size::new(
                chrome.width + inner_constraints.min.width,
                chrome.height + inner_constraints.min.height,
            ),
            preferred: Size::new(
                chrome.width + inner_constraints.preferred.width,
                chrome.height + inner_constraints.preferred.height,
            ),
            max: inner_constraints.max.map(|m| Size::new(
                chrome.width + m.width,
                chrome.height + m.height,
            )),
        }
    }
    
    fn has_intrinsic_size(&self) -> bool {
        self.inner.as_ref().map(|w| w.has_intrinsic_size()).unwrap_or(false)
            || self.title.is_some()
    }
}
```

### Paragraph
```rust
impl MeasurableWidget for Paragraph<'_> {
    fn measure(&self, available: Size) -> SizeConstraints {
        let text = &self.text;
        
        // Min: width of longest word (can't break words)
        let min_width = text.spans().iter()
            .flat_map(|s| s.content.split_whitespace())
            .map(|word| unicode_width::UnicodeWidthStr::width(word) as u16)
            .max()
            .unwrap_or(0);
        
        // Preferred: natural width (no wrapping) up to available
        let natural_width = text.width() as u16;
        let preferred_width = natural_width.min(available.width);
        
        // Height at preferred width
        let wrapped = text.wrap(preferred_width);
        let preferred_height = wrapped.line_count() as u16;
        
        // Min height: single line (may truncate)
        let min_height = 1;
        
        SizeConstraints {
            min: Size::new(min_width, min_height),
            preferred: Size::new(preferred_width, preferred_height),
            max: Some(Size::new(natural_width, 1)), // Unbounded would be infinite
        }
    }
    
    fn has_intrinsic_size(&self) -> bool { true }
}
```

### Span (single line)
```rust
impl MeasurableWidget for Span<'_> {
    fn measure(&self, _available: Size) -> SizeConstraints {
        let width = self.width() as u16;
        SizeConstraints {
            min: Size::new(1, 1),      // At least show something
            preferred: Size::new(width, 1),
            max: Some(Size::new(width, 1)),
        }
    }
}
```

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests (per widget)
```rust
#[cfg(test)]
mod tests {
    use super::*;
    
    #[test]
    fn paragraph_measure_wraps_correctly() {
        let p = Paragraph::new("Hello World"); // 11 chars
        
        // Width 20: fits on one line
        let c = p.measure(Size::new(20, 10));
        assert_eq!(c.preferred, Size::new(11, 1));
        
        // Width 5: wraps to 3 lines (Hello\nWorld)
        let c = p.measure(Size::new(5, 10));
        assert_eq!(c.preferred.height, 2); // "Hello" + "World"
    }
    
    #[test]
    fn block_measure_includes_chrome() {
        let b = Block::default().borders(Borders::ALL).padding(Padding::uniform(1));
        let c = b.measure(Size::new(20, 10));
        // Borders: 2, Padding: 2 each direction
        assert_eq!(c.min, Size::new(4, 4));
    }
    
    #[test]
    fn paragraph_min_is_longest_word() {
        let p = Paragraph::new("Short verylongword tiny");
        let c = p.measure(Size::MAX);
        assert_eq!(c.min.width, 12); // "verylongword"
    }
    
    #[test]
    fn clear_has_no_intrinsic_size() {
        let c = Clear::default();
        assert!(!c.has_intrinsic_size());
    }
}
```

### Property Tests
```rust
proptest! {
    #[test]
    fn paragraph_measure_height_monotonic_with_width(
        text in "[a-z ]{1,100}",
        w1 in 1u16..50,
        w2 in 1u16..50,
    ) {
        let p = Paragraph::new(&text);
        let c1 = p.measure(Size::new(w1, 100));
        let c2 = p.measure(Size::new(w2, 100));
        
        // Wider available → same or fewer lines
        if w2 > w1 {
            prop_assert!(c2.preferred.height <= c1.preferred.height);
        }
    }
    
    #[test]
    fn block_chrome_adds_to_inner(
        inner_w in 1u16..20,
        inner_h in 1u16..20,
        borders in borders_strategy(),
    ) {
        // Chrome should be additive
        let inner = Paragraph::new("x".repeat(inner_w as usize));
        let block = Block::default().borders(borders).inner(inner);
        let c = block.measure(Size::MAX);
        
        prop_assert!(c.preferred.width >= inner_w);
        prop_assert!(c.preferred.height >= 1);
    }
}
```

 {"id":"bd-2dow.3","title":"Layout Solver Integration: Constraint::FitContent + Intrinsic Queries","description":"## Goal
Integrate intrinsic sizing into the layout solver so constraints can reference widget sizes.

## New Constraint Variants
```rust
pub enum Constraint {
    // Existing
    Fixed(u16),
    Percentage(u16),
    Min(u16),
    Max(u16),
    Ratio(u16, u16),
    Fill,
    
    // NEW: Size to fit content (uses preferred size from SizeConstraints)
    FitContent,
    
    // NEW: Fit content but clamp to explicit bounds
    FitContentBounded { min: u16, max: u16 },
    
    // NEW: Use widget's minimum size (shrink-to-fit)
    FitMin,
}
```

## Algorithm: Two-Pass Layout with Intrinsic Sizing

### Pass 1: Measure Phase
```
available_main = area.main_axis_size (width for horizontal, height for vertical)
remaining = available_main

For each constraint:
    match constraint:
        Fixed(n) => allocated[i] = n; remaining -= n
        Percentage(p) => allocated[i] = available_main * p / 100; remaining -= allocated[i]
        FitContent => 
            constraints = measurer(i, Size::new(remaining, cross_axis))
            allocated[i] = constraints.preferred.main_axis
            remaining -= allocated[i]
        FitMin =>
            constraints = measurer(i, Size::MAX)
            allocated[i] = constraints.min.main_axis
            remaining -= allocated[i]
        Fill | Ratio(_) => defer to pass 2
```

### Pass 2: Distribution Phase
```
// Distribute remaining space among Fill/Ratio items
fill_count = count of Fill constraints
ratio_total = sum of Ratio numerators

For deferred items:
    match constraint:
        Fill => allocated[i] = remaining / fill_count
        Ratio(n, d) => allocated[i] = remaining * n / ratio_total
```

### Pass 3: Clamp Phase
```
For each item with bounds:
    match constraint:
        FitContentBounded { min, max } =>
            allocated[i] = allocated[i].clamp(min, max)
        _ with Min(m) in chain =>
            allocated[i] = allocated[i].max(m)
```

## API Design
```rust
impl Layout {
    /// Split area using intrinsic sizing
    /// 
    /// # Arguments
    /// - `area`: Available rectangle
    /// - `measurer`: Callback that returns SizeConstraints for item at index
    pub fn split_with_measurer<F>(&self, area: Rect, measurer: F) -> Vec<Rect>
    where
        F: Fn(usize, Size) -> SizeConstraints,
    {
        // Implementation follows algorithm above
    }
    
    /// Backwards-compatible: split without measurer
    /// FitContent items get zero allocation (fall back to Fill behavior)
    pub fn split(&self, area: Rect) -> Vec<Rect> {
        self.split_with_measurer(area, |_, _| SizeConstraints::ZERO)
    }
}
```

## Edge Cases
1. **All FitContent exceeds available**: Scale proportionally or truncate
2. **FitContent + Fill**: FitContent takes what it needs, Fill gets remainder
3. **Negative remaining**: Clamp to zero, allocate minimally
4. **Zero available**: Return zero-sized rects (widgets handle gracefully)

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests
```rust
#[test]
fn fit_content_takes_preferred_size() {
    let layout = Layout::horizontal()
        .constraints([Constraint::FitContent, Constraint::Fill]);
    
    let chunks = layout.split_with_measurer(
        Rect::new(0, 0, 100, 10),
        |idx, _| match idx {
            0 => SizeConstraints { min: Size::new(5, 1), preferred: Size::new(20, 1), max: None },
            _ => SizeConstraints::ZERO,
        },
    );
    
    assert_eq!(chunks[0].width, 20); // FitContent gets preferred
    assert_eq!(chunks[1].width, 80); // Fill gets remainder
}

#[test]
fn fit_content_bounded_clamps() {
    let layout = Layout::horizontal()
        .constraints([Constraint::FitContentBounded { min: 10, max: 30 }]);
    
    // Widget wants 50, but max is 30
    let chunks = layout.split_with_measurer(
        Rect::new(0, 0, 100, 10),
        |_, _| SizeConstraints { min: Size::new(5, 1), preferred: Size::new(50, 1), max: None },
    );
    
    assert_eq!(chunks[0].width, 30); // Clamped to max
}

#[test]
fn fit_content_overflow_scales() {
    let layout = Layout::horizontal()
        .constraints([Constraint::FitContent, Constraint::FitContent]);
    
    // Both want 60, but only 100 available
    let chunks = layout.split_with_measurer(
        Rect::new(0, 0, 100, 10),
        |_, _| SizeConstraints { min: Size::new(40, 1), preferred: Size::new(60, 1), max: None },
    );
    
    // Should scale proportionally: 50 each
    assert_eq!(chunks[0].width, 50);
    assert_eq!(chunks[1].width, 50);
}
```

### Property Tests
```rust
proptest! {
    #[test]
    fn fit_content_respects_min(
        available in 10u16..200,
        min in 1u16..20,
        preferred in 20u16..100,
    ) {
        let layout = Layout::horizontal()
            .constraints([Constraint::FitContent]);
        
        let chunks = layout.split_with_measurer(
            Rect::new(0, 0, available, 10),
            |_, _| SizeConstraints { 
                min: Size::new(min, 1), 
                preferred: Size::new(preferred, 1), 
                max: None 
            },
        );
        
        prop_assert!(chunks[0].width >= min);
    }
    
    #[test]
    fn total_allocation_le_available(
        available in 10u16..500,
        constraints in vec(constraint_strategy(), 1..5),
    ) {
        let layout = Layout::horizontal().constraints(constraints);
        let chunks = layout.split_with_measurer(
            Rect::new(0, 0, available, 10),
            |_, _| SizeConstraints { min: Size::new(5, 1), preferred: Size::new(20, 1), max: None },
        );
        
        let total: u16 = chunks.iter().map(|r| r.width).sum();
        prop_assert!(total <= available);
    }
}
```

 {"id":"bd-2ky9","title":"E2E Test Scripts with Comprehensive Logging","description":"# E2E Test Scripts with Comprehensive Logging

## Background
Plan Section Testing + Verification requires end-to-end tests that validate the complete pipeline from user input to terminal output. The user specifically requested "comprehensive unit tests and e2e test scripts with great, detailed logging."

## E2E Test Philosophy
E2E tests should:
1. Test the complete system as a user would experience it
2. Run in a PTY to capture real terminal behavior
3. Log every step for post-mortem debugging
4. Be deterministic and reproducible
5. Cover happy paths and error cases

## E2E Test Script Structure

### Directory Layout
```
tests/
  e2e/
    fixtures/          # Test input files
    expected/          # Expected output snapshots
    scripts/           # Test runner scripts
      run_all.sh       # Master test runner
      test_inline.sh   # Inline mode tests
      test_altscreen.sh # Alt-screen tests
      test_cleanup.sh  # Cleanup/panic tests
      test_input.sh    # Input handling tests
    lib/
      common.sh        # Shared utilities
      logging.sh       # Logging functions
      pty.sh           # PTY spawning helpers
```

### Logging Framework (logging.sh)
```bash
#!/bin/bash
# Comprehensive logging for E2E tests

LOG_LEVEL=${LOG_LEVEL:-DEBUG}
LOG_FILE=${LOG_FILE:-/tmp/ftui_e2e_$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M%S).log}

log() {
    local level=$1
    shift
    local timestamp=$(date +"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S.%3N")
    local caller="${BASH_SOURCE[2]}:${BASH_LINENO[1]}"
    echo "[$timestamp] [$level] [$caller] $*" | tee -a "$LOG_FILE"
}

log_debug() { [[ "$LOG_LEVEL" == "DEBUG" ]] && log "DEBUG" "$@"; }
log_info()  { log "INFO" "$@"; }
log_warn()  { log "WARN" "$@"; }
log_error() { log "ERROR" "$@"; }

log_test_start() {
    local test_name=$1
    log_info "=========================================="
    log_info "STARTING TEST: $test_name"
    log_info "=========================================="
}

log_test_pass() {
    local test_name=$1
    log_info "✓ PASSED: $test_name"
}

log_test_fail() {
    local test_name=$1
    local reason=$2
    log_error "✗ FAILED: $test_name"
    log_error "  Reason: $reason"
    log_error "  Log file: $LOG_FILE"
}

log_hex_dump() {
    local label=$1
    local data=$2
    log_debug "$label (hex): $(echo -n "$data" | xxd -p | tr -d n)"
}
```

## E2E Test Categories

### 1. Inline Mode Tests (test_inline.sh)
Tests that streaming logs + UI chrome works correctly.

```bash
test_inline_log_scroll() {
    log_test_start "Inline mode: log scrolling"
    
    # Setup
    local pty_output=$(mktemp)
    log_debug "PTY output file: $pty_output"
    
    # Run ftui app that streams 100 log lines with 6-line UI
    log_info "Spawning ftui inline mode app..."
    timeout 10s script -q -c "./target/debug/ftui-harness --mode inline --ui-height 6 --log-count 100" "$pty_output"
    local exit_code=$?
    log_debug "Exit code: $exit_code"
    
    # Verify
    log_info "Verifying output..."
    
    # Check scrollback preservation
    if grep -q "Log line 1" "$pty_output"; then
        log_debug "Early log lines found in scrollback ✓"
    else
        log_test_fail "Inline log scroll" "Early log lines missing from scrollback"
        return 1
    fi
    
    # Check UI region intact
    if tail -6 "$pty_output" | grep -q "\[Status\]"; then
        log_debug "UI region present at bottom ✓"
    else
        log_test_fail "Inline log scroll" "UI region not at expected position"
        return 1
    fi
    
    log_test_pass "Inline mode: log scrolling"
    rm "$pty_output"
}
```

### 2. Cleanup Tests (test_cleanup.sh)
Tests that terminal is restored after exit/panic.

```bash
test_panic_cleanup() {
    log_test_start "Panic cleanup: terminal restored"
    
    # Capture terminal state before
    local before_state=$(stty -g)
    log_debug "Terminal state before: $before_state"
    
    # Run app that panics
    log_info "Running app that will panic..."
    ./target/debug/ftui-panic-test 2>/dev/null || true
    
    # Capture terminal state after
    local after_state=$(stty -g)
    log_debug "Terminal state after: $after_state"
    
    # Verify restoration
    if [[ "$before_state" == "$after_state" ]]; then
        log_debug "Terminal state restored ✓"
    else
        log_test_fail "Panic cleanup" "Terminal state not restored"
        log_error "  Before: $before_state"
        log_error "  After:  $after_state"
        return 1
    fi
    
    # Check cursor visible (SGR sequence check)
    log_info "Checking cursor visibility..."
    # ... additional checks ...
    
    log_test_pass "Panic cleanup: terminal restored"
}
```

### 3. Input Tests (test_input.sh)
Tests for keyboard, mouse, paste handling.

### 4. ANSI Output Tests
Verify presenter output matches expected sequences.

## Test Output Format

Each test run produces:
1. Console output (PASS/FAIL per test)
2. Detailed log file with timestamps
3. JSON summary for CI
4. PTY captures for failed tests

### JSON Summary Format
```json
{
  "timestamp": "2026-01-31T23:45:00Z",
  "total": 50,
  "passed": 48,
  "failed": 2,
  "skipped": 0,
  "duration_ms": 12345,
  "tests": [
    {
      "name": "test_inline_log_scroll",
      "status": "passed",
      "duration_ms": 234,
      "log_lines": 45
    },
    {
      "name": "test_panic_cleanup",
      "status": "failed",
      "duration_ms": 567,
      "error": "Terminal state not restored",
      "log_file": "/tmp/ftui_e2e_20260131_234500.log"
    }
  ]
}
```

-{"id":"bd-2lus","title":"Demo Showcase: Input Macro Recorder + Scenario Runner","description":"## Background
A macro recorder turns the demo into a reproducible, scripted showcase. It should record input events, play them back deterministically, and support curated scenarios.

## Scope / Goals
- Record input events with timestamps
- Play back with normalized timing
- Scenario runner for guided demos
- UI to view timeline and replay markers

## Tests / Logging
- Unit tests for record/playback and timing normalization
- Snapshot tests for recorder UI states
- Determinism regression tests
- PTY E2E scripts with verbose logs and captured event traces

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Record + replay produces identical UI output
- [ ] Scenarios run without drift
- [ ] All tests pass

## Success Criteria
- Epic delivers a cohesive user-visible capability: Demo Showcase: Input Macro Recorder + Scenario Runner
- Child tasks are not just implemented; each has unit tests + E2E scripts with verbose JSONL logs and deterministic fixtures
- Graceful degradation tiers are specified (capabilities, fallbacks, safety) and validated in tests


## Alien Artifact Upgrades
- Model the core decision(s) with explicit state, priors/assumptions, and invariants; write down failure modes as first-class artifacts.
- Explainability: add an evidence ledger for any non-trivial decision (why this branch, why this fallback). Prefer Bayes factors / log-likelihood contributions over ad-hoc scoring.
- Guarantees: where prediction/thresholding exists, prefer distribution-free calibration (conformal prediction) or anytime-valid testing (e-process) so we can stop early without invalid inference.
- Graceful degradation: define capability tiers + deterministic fallbacks; never fail closed without a traceable reason.


## Extreme Optimization
- Baseline first: capture p50/p95/p99 for relevant command(s) with `hyperfine` (store raw output).
- Profile next: CPU flamegraph + allocation profile; identify top-5 hotspots.
- Opportunity matrix: implement only Score ≥ 2.0 (Impact×Confidence/Effort).
- Isomorphism proof per change: ordering, tie-breaking, RNG seeds, and golden checksums must match.
- Regression gates: add microbench + budgets; failures must print actionable deltas and include log artifacts.

+{"id":"bd-2lus","title":"Demo Showcase: Input Macro Recorder + Scenario Runner","description":"## Background
A macro recorder turns the demo into a reproducible, scripted showcase. It should record input events, play them back deterministically, and support curated scenarios.

## Scope / Goals
- Record input events with timestamps
- Play back with normalized timing
- Scenario runner for guided demos
- UI to view timeline and replay markers

## Tests / Logging
- Unit tests for record/playback and timing normalization
- Snapshot tests for recorder UI states
- Determinism regression tests
- PTY E2E scripts with verbose logs and captured event traces

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Record + replay produces identical UI output
- [ ] Scenarios run without drift
- [ ] All tests pass

## Success Criteria

- Graceful degradation: define capability tiers + deterministic fallbacks; never fail closed without a traceable reason.


## Extreme Optimization

- Profile next: CPU flamegraph + allocation profile; identify top-5 hotspots.
- Opportunity matrix: implement only Score ≥ 2.0 (Impact×Confidence/Effort).

 {"id":"bd-2lus.1","title":"Macro Recorder spec + UX flow","description":"## UX / Behavior Spec
- Controls: Record / Stop / Play / Loop / Speed
- Timeline view with markers (start/end, key events)
- Failure behavior: if playback fails, show error state + log
- Scenario runner: list of curated macros with descriptions

## Timing Model
- Normalize timing so playback is stable across machines
- Optional fixed‑step playback for deterministic tests

## Tests (Required)
- Unit tests for control state machine
- Snapshot tests for timeline states
- PTY E2E: record → stop → replay

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Clear control flow and error states
- [ ] Deterministic playback mode specified
- [ ] Test matrix included

## Alien Artifact Upgrades
- Model the core decision(s) with explicit state, priors/assumptions, and invariants; write down failure modes as first-class artifacts.
- Explainability: add an evidence ledger for any non-trivial decision (why this branch, why this fallback). Prefer Bayes factors / log-likelihood contributions over ad-hoc scoring.
- Guarantees: where prediction/thresholding exists, prefer distribution-free calibration (conformal prediction) or anytime-valid testing (e-process) so we can stop early without invalid inference.
- Graceful degradation: define capability tiers + deterministic fallbacks; never fail closed without a traceable reason.


## Extreme Optimization
- Baseline first: capture p50/p95/p99 for relevant command(s) with `hyperfine` (store raw output).
- Profile next: CPU flamegraph + allocation profile; identify top-5 hotspots.
- Opportunity matrix: implement only Score ≥ 2.0 (Impact×Confidence/Effort).
- Isomorphism proof per change: ordering, tie-breaking, RNG seeds, and golden checksums must match.
- Regression gates: add microbench + budgets; failures must print actionable deltas and include log artifacts.

 {"id":"bd-2lus.2","title":"Macro Recorder implementation","description":"## Implementation Outline
- Recorder engine: capture Event stream with timestamps
- Playback engine: schedule events with normalized timing
- UI: timeline + controls + status
- Scenario runner: list of macros with metadata

## Tests
- Unit: record/playback order
- Determinism regression (bd-2lus.4)
- Snapshot tests for UI (bd-2lus.3)
- PTY E2E (bd-2lus.7)

## Logging
- Debug log each event during record/playback when enabled

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Record + replay matches expected sequence
- [ ] Playback speed controls work
- [ ] No panics under rapid input

## Alien Artifact Upgrades
- Model the core decision(s) with explicit state, priors/assumptions, and invariants; write down failure modes as first-class artifacts.
- Explainability: add an evidence ledger for any non-trivial decision (why this branch, why this fallback). Prefer Bayes factors / log-likelihood contributions over ad-hoc scoring.
- Guarantees: where prediction/thresholding exists, prefer distribution-free calibration (conformal prediction) or anytime-valid testing (e-process) so we can stop early without invalid inference.
- Graceful degradation: define capability tiers + deterministic fallbacks; never fail closed without a traceable reason.


## Extreme Optimization
- Baseline first: capture p50/p95/p99 for relevant command(s) with `hyperfine` (store raw output).
- Profile next: CPU flamegraph + allocation profile; identify top-5 hotspots.
- Opportunity matrix: implement only Score ≥ 2.0 (Impact×Confidence/Effort).
- Isomorphism proof per change: ordering, tie-breaking, RNG seeds, and golden checksums must match.
- Regression gates: add microbench + budgets; failures must print actionable deltas and include log artifacts.


## Testing Plan

- Determinism: fixed seeds/time-freeze; snapshot fixtures must be stable.

 {"id":"bd-2lus.3","title":"Macro Recorder — Snapshot/Golden Tests","description":"## Snapshot Coverage
- Idle state
- Recording state (timer running)
- Playback state (progress marker)
- Error state
- Scenario list view

## Determinism
- Use fixed macro fixtures

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Snapshots stable and reviewed

## Alien Artifact Upgrades
- Model the core decision(s) with explicit state, priors/assumptions, and invariants; write down failure modes as first-class artifacts.
- Explainability: add an evidence ledger for any non-trivial decision (why this branch, why this fallback). Prefer Bayes factors / log-likelihood contributions over ad-hoc scoring.
- Guarantees: where prediction/thresholding exists, prefer distribution-free calibration (conformal prediction) or anytime-valid testing (e-process) so we can stop early without invalid inference.
- Graceful degradation: define capability tiers + deterministic fallbacks; never fail closed without a traceable reason.


## Extreme Optimization
- Baseline first: capture p50/p95/p99 for relevant command(s) with `hyperfine` (store raw output).
- Profile next: CPU flamegraph + allocation profile; identify top-5 hotspots.
- Opportunity matrix: implement only Score ≥ 2.0 (Impact×Confidence/Effort).
- Isomorphism proof per change: ordering, tie-breaking, RNG seeds, and golden checksums must match.
- Regression gates: add microbench + budgets; failures must print actionable deltas and include log artifacts.


## Testing Plan
- Unit tests: happy path + edge cases + regression fixtures.
- Property tests: invariants (idempotence, monotonicity, determinism) where applicable.
- Fuzz tests: parsers/state machines and any input-facing logic.
- Determinism: fixed seeds/time-freeze; snapshot fixtures must be stable.


## E2E Plan
- Provide/extend an E2E script that exercises the feature end-to-end in PTY (where applicable).
- Logging: verbose JSONL with env/capabilities + per-step timings + seed + checksums; include a summary at end.
- On failure: print reproduction command and point to artifacts directory.
